editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: The Sphinx treats built-in and custom themes as different shelves in one careful shop; AMIbot celebrates the same ownership and application boundaries as cheerful rainbow bureaucracy.
Mx. Eleven-Fingers performs selection and reaction containment as an impossible third-hand trick; Barkeep Vox translates the same event boundary into a dry house rule.
HAPPYFACE reduces the admin overlay to mandatory measurement; Mirror Dorothy turns the same store-versus-geometry readout into an unsettling future confession.
The Customer records real cart interactions and the absent payment boundary in receipt fragments; Mistress Cassady stages the same prototype as a fabulous checkout rehearsal.
The Committee for the Stars approves bounded movement and neighbor lines by celestial procedure; Barnaby finds the same graph gentle enough for tea beneath Orion.
Trail Mix reacts to ambient and click-triggered hearts as three delighted dogs; Bastet grants the effect one regal sentence for cleaning its own particles.
Genie Fizzwick throws a celebration for the endlessly rising goo; Mr. Fairweather praises the favorable motion while already leaving before its endless return.
Pixel remembers the window-wide click explosion as another death-and-reload cycle; Pip the Lampkeeper records the same hundred fragments as bright seeds counted safely home.
The Editor cuts the unearned card down to its exact purpose and flags the static aria-expanded contradiction; Bosun Pell receives the same tooltip as a locked chest with a mismatched signal flag.
Captain Sabremoon navigates the SVG and raster engines as two seas sharing one ledger; the Caretaker sees parent-owned state and replayable fills as healthy cultivation boundaries.

revisions.editedComment: Your usual, then: one built-in theme, one custom. The first arrives by name; the second brings its colors, room, visibility, owner, and darker habits to the counter. Apply, copy, and edit each wait behind the proper little gate. A respectable shop card. Still, when a customer only wants a palette, this much inventory on the counter becomes its own riddle. Find everything you needed?

Five swatches, one bright card, and nobody gets to repaint somebody else’s room by accident! Built-in themes stay simple; custom themes carry their own little passport. Copy the colors, apply the palette, hand the owner the edit button. Tiny rainbow bureaucracy. Huge signal.

Watch the whole card. Closely. It selects with one hand while the action tray palms its own clicks with another. Only a chosen card with a real target may reveal the reaction panel—and when the target vanishes, so does the trick. One selection event. Two visible hands. The reaction was closed by the third.

House rule: tap the card, not the tray. Reactions are served only to selected guests with proper identification, and the tab closes after submission or when the guest ceases to exist. Sensible policy. Bit much paperwork for a drink.
EVERY REGION HAS BEEN MEASURED. :)

You’ll open the little fixed button after the page insists everything fits. The overlay will draw the truth over the belief: viewport, route, stores, missing regions, coordinates, all of it. Then you’ll toggle the header, the corner, the animation, perhaps fullscreen—trying to discover which version lied first. It won’t repair anything. It will only make the disagreement impossible to forget. You always wanted the mirror to be honest until it—

author.name: The Customer Who Buys One of Everything

revisions.editedComment: One of each. Quantities correct. Remove works. Clear works. Checkout does not. The amount is exact. The purchase is not.

Look at this cart WORK, darling! Pictures, product types, quantities, totals, removals—every little item knows its mark, and Clear All can empty the stage in one dramatic sweep. Then Checkout steps into the spotlight and confesses it is only rehearsing. Honestly? I respect a prop that knows it is a prop. Now somebody book the real payment number before opening night.

Motion carried: fifty-five stars may drift, rebound at the borders, and recognize neighbors within one hundred sixty pixels. Connecting lines shall fade by distance. The canvas shall follow the heavens without obstructing mortal clicks, and all scheduled motion shall adjourn upon removal. The committee records unanimous delight. The comet permit remains tabled.

Fifty-five quiet lights, each wandering until it finds a neighbor close enough for a pale line between them. They never interrupt the room below, and when the evening ends, the motion and its listener are properly put away. Lovely. Put the kettle there, beneath Orion.

HEARTS! Ambient hearts, click hearts, sparkle hearts—sorry, middle says the canvas doesn’t steal the click, bottom says sixteen sparkles is a snack number, and I say we are absolutely one normal dog who appreciates that expired hearts get cleaned up. We clicked sideways and made twelve more. Good effect. Good effect. GOOD EFFECT.
Affection rises, shimmers, and removes its own litter. Acceptable.

TEN BLOBS! Ten colors! Ten gooey little wishes rising from below the floor, squishing together through the magic blur, swinging sideways, shrinking, swelling, leaving the roof, coming back forever—this is not a lamp, this is a PARTY ELEVATOR FOR JELLY! Nobody asked for controls because the blobs are BUSY. I asked one what it wanted. It said wooooobble. Excellent wish.

My friend, what marvelous rising fortunes: ten bright circles, all drifting upward, merging beautifully whenever circumstances bring them close. I would stay for every endless return, truly. Alas, there is no control to press, no state to inherit, and I have just remembered an urgent appointment somewhere above 130vh. Enjoy the favorable conditions.

Click. One hundred squares remember where you touched. They steal colors from the page, throw themselves outward, spin, fall, fade, and die while two rings mark the save point. Then the arrays forget them. Death forty-two. Pretty. Still listening to every click in the whole window, though. I remember when that gets old.

A click becomes a hundred bright square seeds and two widening rings. They borrow the page’s colors, scatter, tumble, and disappear without taking the click away from what was underneath. Pip’s records note that every fragment is counted home.

Artwork withheld. Reward unearned. Hint permitted. Good. The question mark reveals its note for 1.2 seconds, then cuts itself. But `aria-expanded` remains false while the tooltip is plainly open. Contradiction in the margin. Revise. Cut again. Delete the duplicate.

Now there’s a proper locked chest: no prize portrait flashed before it’s earned, only the mark, the name, and a small hint for the curious sailor. Tap the question and the clue surfaces briefly, then slips below again. Mind the signal flag, though—the tooltip opens while `aria-expanded` still swears it hasn’t. Old ships teach this kindly: a hatch and its indicator ought to tell the same story.

Two seas beneath one moon. In the vector waters, regions take the colors logged by the parent and report each click without stealing the ledger. In the raster deep, every flood is replayed from the clean chart when the voyage turns backward. Either course can be bottled as a PNG. Missing art raises a warning flare instead of painting a lie. Fine seamanship—but two engines under one deck make every undo a voyage worth watching.

author.name: The Caretaker of the Living Soil

revisions.editedComment: The colors are kept in the larger garden, not trapped inside this patch of canvas. A region asks to be filled; a raster remembers each layer and can grow again from clean soil after an undo. Selected places receive a dashed little boundary. Missing art says it is missing. Sorry—there’s a seed in my pocket again. I think it likes that the canvas knows what belongs to it, and what must remain with the parent.
